result.markdown:
--- title: "Data transformation with dplyr" subtitle: "Day 2 - Introduction to Data Analysis with R" author: "Selina Baldauf" institute: "Freie Universität Berlin - Theoretical Ecology" date: today date-format: long format: revealjs: footer: "Selina Baldauf // Data transformation with dplyr" highlight-style: breeze mainfont: Cabinet Grotesk slide-number: true show-slide-number: all incremental: true self-contained: true code-line-numbers: false auto-stretch: false scrollable: false theme: slides.scss fig-align: center fig-width: 6.3 fig-height: 6.3 execute: echo: true warning: false message: false cache: true knitr: opts_chunk: collapse: true comment: "#>" from: markdown+emoji filters: - roughnotation - flourish --- ## Data transformation Data transformation is an important step in **understanding** the data and **preparing** it for further analysis. ![](img/day1/datascience_workflow_tidyverse.png)
We can use the tidyverse package `dplyr` for this. ## Data transformation With `dplyr` we can (among other things) :::{.nonincremental} - **Filter** data to analyse only a part of it - **Create** new variables - **Summarize** data - **Combine** multiple tables - **Rename** variables - **Reorder** observations or variables ::: . . . To get started load the package `dplyr`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} library(dplyr) # or library(tidyverse) ``` ::: ## Dplyr basic vocabulary All of the `dplyr` functions work similarly:
- **First argument** is the data (a tibble) - **Other arguments** specify what to do exactly - **Return** a tibble ## The data The `gapminder` dataset contains data on life expectancy, GDP per capita, and population for 142 countries from 1952 to 2007. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # install.packages("gapminder") library(gapminder) gapminder #> # A tibble: 1,704 × 6 #> country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap #> #> 1 Afghanistan Asia 1952 28.8 8425333 779. #> 2 Afghanistan Asia 1957 30.3 9240934 821. #> 3 Afghanistan Asia 1962 32.0 10267083 853. #> 4 Afghanistan Asia 1967 34.0 11537966 836. #> 5 Afghanistan Asia 1972 36.1 13079460 740. #> 6 Afghanistan Asia 1977 38.4 14880372 786. #> 7 Afghanistan Asia 1982 39.9 12881816 978. #> 8 Afghanistan Asia 1987 40.8 13867957 852. #> 9 Afghanistan Asia 1992 41.7 16317921 649. #> 10 Afghanistan Asia 1997 41.8 22227415 635. #> # ℹ 1,694 more rows ``` ::: # `filter()` {.inverse} > picks rows based on their value ## `filter()` Filter only European countries: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} filter(gapminder, continent == "Europe") #> # A tibble: 360 × 6 #> country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap #> #> 1 Albania Europe 1952 55.2 1282697 1601. #> 2 Albania Europe 1957 59.3 1476505 1942. #> 3 Albania Europe 1962 64.8 1728137 2313. #> 4 Albania Europe 1967 66.2 1984060 2760. #> 5 Albania Europe 1972 67.7 2263554 3313. #> 6 Albania Europe 1977 68.9 2509048 3533. #> 7 Albania Europe 1982 70.4 2780097 3631. #> 8 Albania Europe 1987 72 3075321 3739. #> 9 Albania Europe 1992 71.6 3326498 2497. #> 10 Albania Europe 1997 73.0 3428038 3193. #> # ℹ 350 more rows ``` ::: . . . `filter()` goes through each row of the data and returns only those rows where the value for `continent` is `"Europe"` ## `filter()` You can also combine filters using logical operators (`&`, `|`, `!`): ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} filter(gapminder, continent == "Europe" & year == 2007) #> # A tibble: 30 × 6 #> country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap #> #> 1 Albania Europe 2007 76.4 3600523 5937. #> 2 Austria Europe 2007 79.8 8199783 36126. #> 3 Belgium Europe 2007 79.4 10392226 33693. #> 4 Bosnia and Herzegovina Europe 2007 74.9 4552198 7446. #> 5 Bulgaria Europe 2007 73.0 7322858 10681. #> 6 Croatia Europe 2007 75.7 4493312 14619. #> 7 Czech Republic Europe 2007 76.5 10228744 22833. #> 8 Denmark Europe 2007 78.3 5468120 35278. #> 9 Finland Europe 2007 79.3 5238460 33207. #> 10 France Europe 2007 80.7 61083916 30470. #> # ℹ 20 more rows ``` ::: ## `filter()` + `%in%` Use the `%in%` operator to filter rows based on multiple values, e.g. continents . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} my_continents <- c("Europe", "Africa", "Asia") filter(gapminder, continent %in% my_continents) #> # A tibble: 1,380 × 6 #> country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap #> #> 1 Afghanistan Asia 1952 28.8 8425333 779. #> 2 Afghanistan Asia 1957 30.3 9240934 821. #> 3 Afghanistan Asia 1962 32.0 10267083 853. #> 4 Afghanistan Asia 1967 34.0 11537966 836. #> 5 Afghanistan Asia 1972 36.1 13079460 740. #> 6 Afghanistan Asia 1977 38.4 14880372 786. #> 7 Afghanistan Asia 1982 39.9 12881816 978. #> 8 Afghanistan Asia 1987 40.8 13867957 852. #> 9 Afghanistan Asia 1992 41.7 16317921 649. #> 10 Afghanistan Asia 1997 41.8 22227415 635. #> # ℹ 1,370 more rows ``` ::: ## `filter()` + `is.na()` You can use `is.na()` to check for missing values: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Filter rows where lifeExp is missing filter(gapminder, is.na(lifeExp)) ``` ::: . . . Or keep only rows **without** missing values using `filter_out()`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Remove rows where lifeExp is missing filter_out(gapminder, is.na(lifeExp)) ``` ::: . . . The gapminder data has no missing values, so these filters don't change anything here. But this is very useful for real-world data! ## `filter()` + `between()` Filter rows where the value for `year` is between 1980 and 2000 :::{.fragment} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} filter(gapminder, between(year, 1980, 2000)) #> # A tibble: 568 × 6 #> country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap #> #> 1 Afghanistan Asia 1982 39.9 12881816 978. #> 2 Afghanistan Asia 1987 40.8 13867957 852. #> 3 Afghanistan Asia 1992 41.7 16317921 649. #> 4 Afghanistan Asia 1997 41.8 22227415 635. #> 5 Albania Europe 1982 70.4 2780097 3631. #> 6 Albania Europe 1987 72 3075321 3739. #> 7 Albania Europe 1992 71.6 3326498 2497. #> 8 Albania Europe 1997 73.0 3428038 3193. #> 9 Algeria Africa 1982 61.4 20033753 5745. #> 10 Algeria Africa 1987 65.8 23254956 5681. #> # ℹ 558 more rows ``` ::: ::: :::{.fragment} Or you could also do it like this: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} filter(gapminder, year >= 1980 & year <= 2000) ``` ::: ::: ## Useful `filter()` helpers These functions and operators help you filter your observations: :::{.nonincremental} - relational operators `<`, `>`, `==`, ... - logical operators `&`, `|`, `!` - `%in%` to filter multiple values - `is.na()` to filter missing values - `filter_out()` to remove rows matching a condition - `between()` to filter values that are between an upper and lower boundary - `near()` to compare floating points (use instead of `==` for doubles) ::: # `select()` {.inverse} > picks columns based on their names ## `select()` Select the columns `country`, `year`, and `lifeExp` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} select(gapminder, country, year, lifeExp) #> # A tibble: 1,704 × 3 #> country year lifeExp #> #> 1 Afghanistan 1952 28.8 #> 2 Afghanistan 1957 30.3 #> 3 Afghanistan 1962 32.0 #> 4 Afghanistan 1967 34.0 #> 5 Afghanistan 1972 36.1 #> 6 Afghanistan 1977 38.4 #> 7 Afghanistan 1982 39.9 #> 8 Afghanistan 1987 40.8 #> 9 Afghanistan 1992 41.7 #> 10 Afghanistan 1997 41.8 #> # ℹ 1,694 more rows ``` ::: . . . Remove variables using `-` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} select(gapminder, -country, -year, -lifeExp) ``` ::: ## `select()` + `starts_with()` Select all columns that start with `"c"` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} select(gapminder, starts_with("c")) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ``` #> # A tibble: 1,704 × 2 #> country continent #> #> 1 Afghanistan Asia #> 2 Afghanistan Asia #> 3 Afghanistan Asia #> # ℹ 1,701 more rows ``` ::: . . . You can use the same structure for `ends_with()` and `contains()`. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # select columns that end with "Exp" or "cap" select(gapminder, ends_with("Exp")) # select columns that contain "life" select(gapminder, contains("life")) ``` ::: ## `select()` + `from:to` {visibility="hidden"} Multiple consecutive columns can be selected using the `from:to` structure with either column id or name: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} select(gapminder, 1:3) select(gapminder, country:year) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ``` #> # A tibble: 1,704 × 3 #> country continent year #> #> 1 Afghanistan Asia 1952 #> 2 Afghanistan Asia 1957 #> 3 Afghanistan Asia 1962 #> # ℹ 1,701 more rows ``` ::: . . . Be a bit careful with these commands: They are not robust if you e.g. change the order of your columns at some point.
## Useful `select()` helpers :::{.nonincremental} - `starts_with()` and `ends_with()`: variable names that start/end with a specific string - `contains()`: variable names that contain a specific string - `matches()`: variable names that match a regular expression - `any_of()` and `all_of()`: variables that are contained in a character vector ::: # `mutate()` {.inverse} > Adds new columns to your data ## `mutate()` New columns can be added based on values from other columns ::: {.cell flourish='[{"target":"gdp = gdpPercap * pop"}]'} ```{.r .cell-code} mutate(gapminder, gdp = gdpPercap * pop) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ``` #> # A tibble: 1,704 × 7 #> country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap gdp #> #> 1 Afghanistan Asia 1952 28.8 8425333 779. 6567086330. #> 2 Afghanistan Asia 1957 30.3 9240934 821. 7585448670. #> 3 Afghanistan Asia 1962 32.0 10267083 853. 8758855797. #> # ℹ 1,701 more rows ``` ::: . . . Add multiple new columns at once: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mutate(gapminder, gdp = gdpPercap * pop, pop_million = pop / 1e6) ``` ::: ## `mutate()` + `case_when()` Use `case_when` to add column values conditional on other columns. `case_when()` can combine many cases into one. ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} mutate( gapminder, life_category = case_when( lifeExp < 50 ~ "low", # case 1 lifeExp < 70 ~ "medium", # case 2 lifeExp >= 70 ~ "high", # case 3 .default = NA # all other ) ) #> # A tibble: 1,704 × 7 #> country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap life_category #> #> 1 Afghanistan Asia 1952 28.8 8425333 779. low #> 2 Afghanistan Asia 1957 30.3 9240934 821. low #> 3 Afghanistan Asia 1962 32.0 10267083 853. low #> 4 Afghanistan Asia 1967 34.0 11537966 836. low #> 5 Afghanistan Asia 1972 36.1 13079460 740. low #> 6 Afghanistan Asia 1977 38.4 14880372 786. low #> 7 Afghanistan Asia 1982 39.9 12881816 978. low #> 8 Afghanistan Asia 1987 40.8 13867957 852. low #> 9 Afghanistan Asia 1992 41.7 16317921 649. low #> 10 Afghanistan Asia 1997 41.8 22227415 635. low #> # ℹ 1,694 more rows ``` ::: # `summarize()` {.inverse} > summarizes data ## `summarize()` `summarize` will **collapse the data to a single row** . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} summarize( gapminder, mean_lifeExp = mean(lifeExp), mean_gdpPercap = mean(gdpPercap) ) #> # A tibble: 1 × 2 #> mean_lifeExp mean_gdpPercap #> #> 1 59.5 7215. ``` ::: ## `summarize()` by group `summarize` is much more useful in combination with the grouping argument `.by` - **summary** will be calculated **separately for each group** . . . ::: {.cell flourish='[{"target":".by = continent"}]'} ```{.r .cell-code} # summarize the grouped data summarize( gapminder, mean_lifeExp = mean(lifeExp), mean_gdpPercap = mean(gdpPercap), .by = continent ) #> # A tibble: 5 × 3 #> continent mean_lifeExp mean_gdpPercap #> #> 1 Asia 60.1 7902. #> 2 Europe 71.9 14469. #> 3 Africa 48.9 2194. #> 4 Americas 64.7 7136. #> 5 Oceania 74.3 18622. ``` ::: . . . - Combine variables if you want to summarize by more than one group (e.g. `.by = c(continent, year)`) ## `count()` Counts observations by group ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # count rows grouped by continent count(gapminder, continent) #> # A tibble: 5 × 2 #> continent n #> #> 1 Africa 624 #> 2 Americas 300 #> 3 Asia 396 #> 4 Europe 360 #> 5 Oceania 24 ``` ::: # The pipe ` |> ` {.inverse} > Combine multiple data operations into one command ## The pipe `|>` Data transformation often requires **multiple operations** in sequence. The pipe operator `|>` helps to keep these operations clear and readable. - You may also see `%>%` from the `magrittr` package :::{.fragment} Turn on the native R pipe ` |> ` in **Tools -> Global Options -> Code** ![](img/day2/native-pipe.png){width=50%} ::: :::{.aside} See [here](https://www.tidyverse.org/blog/2023/04/base-vs-magrittr-pipe/) for differences between the two pipe versions ::: ## The pipe `|>` Let's look at an example without pipe: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # 1: filter only data from 2007 gapminder_2007 <- filter(gapminder, year == 2007) # 2: count countries by continent gapminder_2007 <- count(gapminder_2007, continent) ``` ::: . . . **How could we make this more efficient?** . . . Use one **nested function** without intermediate results: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder_2007 <- count( filter(gapminder, year == 2007), continent ) ``` ::: . . . But this gets complicated and error prone very quickly ## The pipe `|>` The pipe operator makes it very easy to combine multiple operations: ::: {.cell flourish='[{"target":"|>"}]'} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder_2007 <- gapminder |> filter(year == 2007) |> count(continent) gapminder_2007 ``` ::: . . . You can read from top to bottom and interpret the `|>` as an "and then do". ## The pipe `|>` But what is happening? The pipe is "pushing" the result of one line into the first argument of the function from the next line. . . . ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder |> count(continent) # instead of count(gapminder, continent) ``` ::: . . . Piping works perfectly with the `tidyverse` functions because they are designed to return a tibble **and** take a tibble as first argument. . . . :::{.callout-tip} Use the keyboard shortcut ` Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + M ` to insert ` |> ` ::: ## The pipe `|>` Piping also works well together with `ggplot` ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder |> filter(year == 2007) |> ggplot(aes(x = gdpPercap, y = lifeExp, color = continent)) + geom_point(size = 3) + scale_x_log10() ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](dplyr_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-29-1.png){width=604.8} ::: ::: ## The pipe `|>` You can also combine dplyr operations and ggplot: :::{.columns} :::{.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder |> summarize( mean_lifeExp = mean(lifeExp), .by = c(continent, year) ) |> ggplot( aes( x = year, y = mean_lifeExp, color = continent ) ) + geom_line(linewidth = 1) ``` ::: - Use `|>` between dplyr steps - Switch to `+` once you start the ggplot ::: :::{.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](dplyr_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-31-1.png){width=604.8} ::: ::: ::: ::: # Combining multiple tables{.inverse} ## Combine two tibbles by row `bind_rows` Situation: Two (or more) `tibbles` with the same variables (column names) ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} tbl_a <- gapminder[1:2, ] # first two rows tbl_b <- gapminder[3:nrow(gapminder), ] # the rest ``` :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} tbl_a ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ``` #> # A tibble: 2 × 6 #> country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap #> #> 1 Afghanistan Asia 1952 28.8 8425333 779. #> 2 Afghanistan Asia 1957 30.3 9240934 821. ``` :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} tbl_b ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ``` #> # A tibble: 1,702 × 6 #> country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap #> #> 1 Afghanistan Asia 1962 32.0 10267083 853. #> 2 Afghanistan Asia 1967 34.0 11537966 836. #> # ℹ 1,700 more rows ``` ::: ## Combine two tibbles by row `bind_rows` Bind the rows together with `bind_rows()`: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} bind_rows(tbl_a, tbl_b) ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ``` #> # A tibble: 1,704 × 6 #> country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap #> #> 1 Afghanistan Asia 1952 28.8 8425333 779. #> 2 Afghanistan Asia 1957 30.3 9240934 821. #> # ℹ 1,702 more rows ``` ::: . . . You can also add an ID-column to indicate which line belonged to which table: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} bind_rows(a = tbl_a, b = tbl_b, .id = "id") ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ``` #> # A tibble: 1,704 × 7 #> id country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap #> #> 1 a Afghanistan Asia 1952 28.8 8425333 779. #> 2 a Afghanistan Asia 1957 30.3 9240934 821. #> 3 b Afghanistan Asia 1962 32.0 10267083 853. #> # ℹ 1,701 more rows ``` ::: . . . You can use `bind_rows()` to bind as many tables as you want: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} bind_rows(a = tbl_a, b = tbl_b, c = tbl_c, ..., .id = "id") ``` ::: ## Join tibbles with `left_join()` Situation: Two tables that share some but not all columns. . . . ::: {.cell} ::: ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder ``` ::: ::: {.cell} ``` #> # A tibble: 1,704 × 6 #> country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap #> #> 1 Afghanistan Asia 1952 28.8 8425333 779. #> 2 Afghanistan Asia 1957 30.3 9240934 821. #> # ℹ 1,702 more rows ``` :::
::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # table with more information on the continents continent_info #> # A tibble: 5 × 2 #> continent region #> #> 1 Asia Global East #> 2 Europe Western World #> 3 Africa Global South #> 4 Americas Western Hemisphere #> 5 Oceania Pacific ``` ::: ## Join tibbles with `left_join()` Join the two tables by the common column `continent` ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} left_join(gapminder, continent_info, by = "continent") #> # A tibble: 1,704 × 7 #> country continent year lifeExp pop gdpPercap region #> #> 1 Afghanistan Asia 1952 28.8 8425333 779. Global East #> 2 Afghanistan Asia 1957 30.3 9240934 821. Global East #> 3 Afghanistan Asia 1962 32.0 10267083 853. Global East #> 4 Afghanistan Asia 1967 34.0 11537966 836. Global East #> 5 Afghanistan Asia 1972 36.1 13079460 740. Global East #> 6 Afghanistan Asia 1977 38.4 14880372 786. Global East #> 7 Afghanistan Asia 1982 39.9 12881816 978. Global East #> 8 Afghanistan Asia 1987 40.8 13867957 852. Global East #> 9 Afghanistan Asia 1992 41.7 16317921 649. Global East #> 10 Afghanistan Asia 1997 41.8 22227415 635. Global East #> # ℹ 1,694 more rows ``` ::: . . . `left_join()` means that the resulting tibble will contain all rows of `gapminder`, but not necessarily all rows of `continent_info` (in this case it does though). ## Different `*_join()` functions ![](img/day2/dplyr_join.png){width=70% fig-align="center"} # Summary{.inverse} > Data transformation with dplyr ## Summary I All `dplyr` functions take a tibble as first argument and return a tibble. #### `filter()` :::{.nonincremental} - **pick rows** with helpers - relational and logical operators - `%in%` - `is.na()` and `filter_out()` - `between()` - `near()` ::: ## Summary II :::{.nonincremental} All `dplyr` functions take a tibble as first argument and return a tibble. #### `select()` - **pick columns** with helpers - `starts_with()`, `ends_with()` - `contains()` - `matches()` - `any_of()`, `all_of()` ::: ## Summary III #### `arrange()` :::{.nonincremental} - **change order** of rows (adscending) - or descending with `desc()` #### `mutate()` - **add columns** but keep all columns - `case_when()` for conditional values ::: ## Summary IV :::{.nonincremental} #### `summarize()` - **collapse rows** into one row by some summary - use `.by` argument to summarize by group #### `count` - **count rows** based on a group ::: ## Summary V :::{.nonincremental} #### `bind_rows()` - **combine rows** of multiple tibbles into one - the tibbles need to have the same columns - add an id column with the argument `.id = "id"` - function `bind_cols()` works similarly just for columns #### `left_join()` - **combine tables** based on common columns ::: # Now you {.inverse} [Task (30 min)]{.highlight-blue}
[Transform the penguin data set]{.big-text} **Find the task description [here](https://selinazitrone.github.io/intro-r-data-analysis/sessions/08_dplyr.html)**

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
